narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48.2% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and LSAT score of 35, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at the first decile, and factors like your high family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans. The model weighed these elements against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best forecasts bar exam success. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking; being in the bottom decile there signaled to the model a notable risk, as students who passed typically ranked much higher, around the 6th decile on average. Next in influence, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it was reasonably competitive, though still below the 37.27 average for those who passed. Your undergraduate GPA also helped somewhat, nudging things positively despite being lower than the 3.24 average among successful students. While your high family income is less common among bar passers (only about 8.5% in that group), it wasn't a top driver here, nor were your plans to study full-time, which align well with most who succeed. Overall, your profile compares to admitted students by having strengths in test scores and background that offset some risks, but your low Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest gap compared to their stronger mid-to-upper decile averages. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors beyond these metrics shape real-world outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the passing average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted academic support, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth. You might also explore post-baccalaureate programs to improve your GPA trajectory before reapplying. With focused improvements in these areas, your odds could shift dramatically in the next cycle. Keep pushing—you've got the drive, and this is just one data point on your path.
